summaries: walls-and-methods.mdx is a well-calibrated exposition/bridge page and passes on every major axis. All twelve method scores match their source experiment pages exactly and all use the matched-edges convention; the four wall numbers (73-137 legal pieces, ~17 colours, ~80-cell collapse, rigidity) reconcile with their proven/measured source pages; and the 470 ceiling comparison is apples-to-apples matched-edges per the glossary, not a cross-convention error. Rigor is honestly stated: the interpretive wall-attribution matrix and the 'which wall stops which method' reading are labeled rigor: conjectured and hedged in-body as a reading, so no measured-single result is dressed as proven. The Millilaw parallel-tempering/diversity claim is corroborated on-site (open-problems.mdx cross-links back, plus approaches-map and the parallel-tempering method page). FR and ES siblings are natively written and faithful with zero drift on any number or claim-strength across 8 sampled passages each. Only minor issues: an unsourced '15-basin sweep' figure in the PALIMPSEST line, an unexplained rule for the matrix marks, and a people.mdx link to a non-existent william-millilaw page (out of scope for this page). Full inventory at /tmp/why-review/page-walls-and-methods.md.

rigidity-wall.mdx is a structurally sound, honestly-hedged page: it correctly reserves 'proven' for the closed MIP regions and explicitly demotes the general all-boards claim to a conjecture (lines 106-108), so rigor:proven/tier:1 is defensible as a statement about the specific tested boards and does not promote the conjecture to settled. Its SAT half is fully committed and reproduces exactly against research/topics/rigidity-sat-halo/results/halo-sat.json (467-470, all UNSAT out to radius 2). Two major gaps: (F1) the load-bearing MIP proof table has no committed results file on the site and its source is a GitHub tree, while repro backs only the weaker SAT statement; (F2) the benj39100 annealing numbers (429->432, ~42 edges, 4096 replicas, msg 11902) postdate the archive export and are unverifiable against provided evidence yet are stated as fact. Minors (in the file): a 'three boards' vs 'four basins' wobble, an orphan '~70 s (48 cells)' frontmatter figure with no matching table row (flagged by the 2026-07-17 audit, still unfixed), and process-narration tone. FR and ES siblings are natively written and numerically faithful with zero drift across 6+ sampled passages each; they even reproduce the two minor flaws faithfully.

The page is honestly top-labelled (rigor: conjectured) and carries genuine caveats, so it is not faulted for its measured-single tier. Its central weakness is reproducibility: the three headline cycle numbers (154, 80, 85 cells; 14 cycles) appear nowhere except this page's three language files -- no repro.cmd, no topic, no shipped board pair, no committed results -- violating the site's own contribute.mdx rule that heavy computations ship the board so they can be verified. The committed record-boards topic ships 463/460/458/460 boards, not the 459 or McGavin-469 the cycles are computed between. A secondary issue is that the honest 'three pairs, not proven in general' hedge is buried behind four flat assertions of 154 and is entirely absent from the live SigmaCycleLab component note that ships to every viewer. Internal number consistency, the 470-since-2021 framing, and the FR/ES translations (natively written, faithful on every number and hedge across 7+ sampled passages each) all pass. One minor logical gap: the 470-wall conclusion is argued from 469-anchored cycles without noting the extrapolation.

A clean, honest page. Every load-bearing number verifies against its cited source: archive msg 1947 (Owen's I=(196!*4^196)^(1/392)=17.14 derivation, dated 2007-08-18, byte-level match) and msg 4909 (the 2xL rectangle experiment, table matches rectangle-transition.json exactly: L1=57.75%, L4-15 zero, L16=1/400, L19=8.5%, L21=57.25%, L22=88.33%), plus the exact 17/5 color split from the committed color-split.json (proven-internal). No fabrication, no invented internal result dressed as external. The two substantive issues are both about scope/framing rather than falsehood: (1) repro.cmd reproduces only the 17/5 split, not the titular hardness-peak claim, so contribution:measurement + repro:exact overreach their scope; (2) the title/metaDescription say '17 exactly where hardest' / 'confirmed the peak' while the body correctly hedges to 'around 17 / a band' -- the source article's band caveat was dropped in the MDX. FR and ES siblings are full native rewrites, faithful on every number and every hedge (around 17 / about one solution preserved), with no translation drift detected across sampled passages.

The page is cleanly written, trilingually faithful, and internally arithmetically consistent (h(1)/h(2)/lambda_H are genuinely exact and reproducible; the ~80-cell figure checks out against alpha=0.085; FR/ES carry every number and claim-strength identically with correct locale conventions and no drift). Its defect is rigor calibration on the load-bearing claims: the headline area law rho(n)~exp(-0.085 n^2) and the ~80-cell distinctness collapse are NOT produced by repro.cmd — main.rs computes only the grammar-entropy half — and are carried from the private off-site vault, yet the frontmatter (rigor: measured, repro: exact) and the chart's authority present them as reproducible-here. Two compounding provenance problems: the interactive Lab claims block counts are 'verified in-repo' when the in-repo program computes no such counts, and the site's rendered data file (web/src/data/entropy-area-law.json) diverges from the file repro.cmd regenerates. The reproducible core (matching grammar is rich, positive entropy density) is a strictly weaker statement than the finding the page sells. No translation or numeric-consistency faults; the FR/ES siblings faithfully inherit the same overstatement.

complex-theory.mdx is a strong, well-sourced exposition whose core is genuinely reproducible: the headline 14,702-solutions figure, the funnel curve, and all three regime magnitudes (10^45 plateau, 10^4 collapse) match Peter McGavin's reference C output (msg 11197) and the site's byte-validated TS port exactly, and every cited groups.io message number (5197, 5209, 8125, 8924, 9188, 9686, 9688, 11197) exists and backs its claim. `rigor: conjectured` is well-calibrated (if anything slightly humble). The one real defect is C11: the page says backtrackers spend most time 'below depth 150,' but its own cited source (Joe, msg 11725) says time is spent at depth GREATER than 132/150 — the claim is inverted, the 99%/70% figures are mis-mapped, and Joe is not in `sources:`; both FR and ES faithfully propagate the inverted wording. Numbers are internally consistent across all three languages with no translation drift; the FR/ES are natively written and faithful. Remaining issues are minor (peak-depth 161.8 vs Brendan's E2-specific 160.45; two of four clue puzzles slightly exceed a strict factor-2; NxM/R^2 figures rest on external PDFs not locally verifiable) and are recorded in the report file.

An unusually rigor-clean exposition page. Every load-bearing claim is external/textbook (Demaine NP-completeness, Ansotegui empirical benchmarks, Knuth Algorithm C / XCC, encoding constructions) and correctly attributed, and the one empirical/conjectural claim (few solutions, search drowns in dead ends) is repeatedly hedged and explicitly flagged as 'not a theorem' via the tier-calibration sentence present in all three languages. All numbers cross-check (1.115x10^557 verified, 262k variables, 512 items, 12 interior edges) and are internally consistent; the Blackwood/SAT dead-end claim is backed by the community digests (msg 10056). repro:{kind:prose} is the honest declaration for a page that owns no computation and defers quantitative claims to sibling pages. FR and ES siblings are natively written with correct typography and idiomatic re-expression, faithful on every number and every claim-strength hedge, with zero drift. Only two minor issues: the 10^557 figure is described as the raw arrangement count when it already bakes in border/clue constraints (raw space is 10^661), and the page lacks a rigor: frontmatter field (defensible for exposition). No major or critical findings.

web/content/research/why/index.mdx is a well-calibrated kind:page landing/teaser (no rigor/sources/repro fields, correctly so) that is genuinely load-bearing: 15 inbound links from 4 independent parent pages (research/index, open-problems, build/known-facts, build/approaches-map) across all 3 languages make it the site's canonical front door into the why/ cluster, and its curated 'Where to start' reading path is unique content found nowhere else. Its two substantive factual claims (Selby & Riordan hired to co-design E2; the border/interior colour split being a structural byproduct, not a deliberate scarcity trick) both check out against sourced sibling finding pages (design-recipe.mdx, rare-color-geography.mdx) and against the community archive (msgs 716, 901, 2697, 3373, 8823, plus independent archive corroboration at msg 3297, msg 8342, msg 8050). No tier misrepresentation was found. The one real issue is that the 'Engineered to resist cleverness' section paraphrases those two sourced finding pages almost verbatim without citing them or linking to their evidence, which is a discipline gap relative to the rest of /research/why rather than a correctness problem, and is fixable by compression + link-out rather than by folding the page. FR/ES sampled at 3+ passages each are fully faithful: no mistranslations, no dropped claims, no tier drift, consistent attribution and formatting (one trivial FR/ES quote-mark style inconsistency noted, not a meaning error). Recommendation: keep the page standalone; do not fold it into a parent. Full report written to eternity2/undefined/page-index.md.

Reviewed web/content/research/why/design-recipe.mdx (+ .fr.mdx/.es.mdx). This is a strong page overall: rigor:conjectured/tier:2 is correctly calibrated for a pure archival reconstruction (repro:{kind:prose}, no research/topics/ dir, appropriately), all 20 cited msg numbers resolve to real, on-topic, correctly-dated messages in research/community-exports/messages.jsonl, the load-bearing 17.14 formula and the msg-8823 'safe' quote are verified verbatim, FR/ES are faithful across 5 sampled passages including math and quotes, and the '480 exists' framing is handled correctly. Fold question: does NOT fold — 6 reciprocal inbound links from two sibling why/ pages across all three languages plus a distinct scope earn it standalone status. Two real accuracy defects found, both present identically in all three languages: (1) the closing 'records... none since [2021]' claim is contradicted by the site's own records-timeline.ts, which shows a 2024 470 tie and, more importantly, the strict-5/5 record moving 460→464 in July 2026 — sibling page sigma-cycles.mdx scopes the same claim correctly and should be the model for the fix; (2) the 'aaaa, abab, aabb' symmetric-forms example misstates AABB as rotation-symmetric, an error the cited thread (msgs 8014-8034) itself makes and then explicitly retracts within the same cited range (msg 8028 claims it, msg 8032/8034 correct it) — the page states the pre-correction claim as settled fact. Full report written to eternity2/eternity2/undefined/page-design-recipe.md.

The page is sound and well-calibrated. Core claim (196 interior pieces, 73-137 right-hand partners, 0 forced) is an exact, exhaustive, deterministic Rust computation; I independently re-ran `cargo run --release` in research/topics/no-forced-moves/compute and confirmed a byte-for-byte match against the committed results/partner-counts.json. rigor: proven / tier: 2 is correctly calibrated against sibling pages (forbidden-patterns, rare-color-geography) that use the same convention for exact/exhaustive internal computations. FR and ES translations are faithful across five sampled passages (lead sentence, metaDescription, closing paragraph, stat-card labels, transition paragraph) with no numeric drift (196/73/137 identical in all three) and no rigor-tier slippage. The interactive ForcedMovesLab demo is honestly scoped as a small toy instance illustrating the same mechanism, not a live recomputation on the actual 480-piece board. One minor rigor-calibration gap: the live page generalizes from a single-direction (right-hand) pairwise computation to an unqualified 'no forced moves' claim without restating the directional-symmetry justification that the pipeline's own article.md already has in its Notes section. On the load-bearing question, this page is clearly not a fold candidate: it has roughly 14 distinct inbound-citing pages across both why/ and build/, spanning three locales (40+ link sites), including two structural dependents (a known-facts table row and a walls-and-methods comparison-table column) that require it to keep a stable standalone URL. It functions as a shared atomic fact for the whole site, not supporting detail for any single parent."}

rare-color-geography.mdx is a strong, well-calibrated page: every number traces to a committed, deterministic Rust computation reproducible byte-for-byte via just research-rare-color-geography, and the single external citation (groups.io msg 1947) was verified against the community archive and accurately represents Owen's derivation. The proven rigor label matches site convention for exact own-computations. FR and ES siblings are faithful line-for-line across all three sampled passages, including correctly localized in-page anchor links. It has enough distinct inbound citations (6 pages across 3 site sections) and unique interactive content (RareColorRing, RareColorLab) to remain standalone rather than folding into design-recipe.mdx. The only issues found are external to the page itself: a likely mislinked citation in dead-ends.mdx that should point to design-recipe.mdx instead, and a minor rationale overclaim in design-recipe.mdx's own treatment of the same underlying fact (this page is actually the more careful and accurate of the two). Full report written to /Users/raphaelanjou/Documents/dev-projects/polytech/eternity2/eternity2/page-rare-color-geography.md.

Reviewed web/content/research/why/forbidden-patterns.mdx and its FR/ES siblings against the repo's research/topics/forbidden-patterns pipeline and the community message archive. All headline numbers (39%, 83.26%, 99.72%, the derived '1 in 358', and the 0.28% feasible figure) were independently recomputed from the committed feasibility.json and match the prose and table in all three languages exactly. The Rust source performs a genuine exhaustive enumeration (no sampling), matching its repro: kind: exact frontmatter. The one external claim (20/289 'void' corner-color-pairs, cited to msg 5027) was verified against ../research/community-exports/messages.jsonl -- msg_num 5027, dated 2008-04-09, content matches the paraphrase precisely -- and is correctly tiered as proven-external, clearly distinguished from the page's own proven-internal exact counts. Rigor calibration is exemplary throughout: the back-of-envelope 1/17 estimate is repeatedly self-hedged as crude/order-of-magnitude before the exact count is given, and the 'forbidden count as tiebreak' trend claim is backed by a live, visitor-reproducible in-browser computation (ForbiddenCountLab) rather than asserted as static fact. FR and ES translations are faithful across all sampled passages (opening escalation, why-it-matters, the 0.28% derivation, and the msg-5027 citation), with only a harmless idiom variance ('a couple of dozen' vs 'una veintena' vs 'un par de docenas'). Load-bearing verdict: this page clearly stands alone -- at least 7 distinct pages across languages cite it via related: frontmatter or inline links (including known-facts.mdx using it as the canonical detail page for three separate facts), and no other page duplicates its numbers or method, so no fold is warranted. Only a cosmetic ES markdown-table alignment nit was found; no rigor, numeric, or translation defects.

hint-geometry.mdx is a well-scoped, honestly-hedged community-attribution page: the rigor: measured badge, repro: kind: prose convention, and the closing scope disclaimer (generated 16x16 vs official puzzle) are all correctly calibrated, and FR/ES translations are faithful across all sampled passages including the epistemic hedges in the attribution Callout. The 18-scattered-hints headline number was verified exactly by decoding the raw bucas board_pieces param in the cited msg 11746, matching the page prose and the HintGeometryDiagram component's hardcoded coordinates cell-for-cell. However, the page's other headline number, "88+ contiguous hints," is not supported by any of its three cited sources (msg 11725, 11746, 3098); it actually traces to msg 11741 in the same thread, which is uncited, gives no wall-clock time (only iteration counts), and explicitly frames the 88-hint solve as a lucky crypto-RNG find "which I haven't yet hit with the normal RNG" -- materially weaker evidentiary footing than the clean, deterministic 18-hint solve it's directly compared against in the lede's "to be that easy" framing. A second, smaller claim ("15 hints still worked, several hours") also isn't traceable to any cited source. On the fold-vs-standalone question: only one page cites hint-geometry (the raphael-anjou/hint-study experiment), but that citation is substantive -- hint-study treats this page's claim as its null hypothesis, tests the exact same two layouts on its own engine, gets an apparently opposite result at short budget, and spends a full section reconciling the two. That relationship argues for keeping hint-geometry standalone rather than folding it into the experiment page, despite narrow inbound-link count.

The core claim of mismatch-geometry.mdx is real and well-built: a live client-side component decodes four actual bucas boards (McGavin-469, Verhaard-467, KEYRING-460, GAUNTLET-458) and bins conflicts per row using the engine's real scoring rule, and every number checked (11 mismatches, row ranges, 249/256) matches across EN/FR/ES with faithful translation. Two real defects survive verification: (1) repro.kind is 'exact' with no cmd/topic, unlike every sibling kind:exact page in why/, which means the site's own /research/build reproduce index silently drops this page while it still displays an 'exact' badge; (2) the inline Zamofing/msg-11901 quote cannot be checked against the in-scope community archive (msg_num tops out at 11823, zero hits for 'Zamofing'), and it's the only citation on the page absent from frontmatter sources. A minor rigor-calibration issue: 'every record board' / 'the real record boards' language outruns the actual n=4 evidence base. The page is heavily inbound-linked from four parent pages across two site sections and hosts unique interactive content, so it clearly stands alone -- no fold recommended. Full report written to eternity2/eternity2/undefined/page-mismatch-geometry.md.

piece-theft.mdx is strong on its core content: the numeric claims (196 interior pieces, 269 occurring demands, 47 single-server demands, mean 2.90 servers) are exact and exhaustive, verified byte-for-byte across research/topics/piece-theft/compute/src/main.rs, the committed results JSON, and the JSON the live chart consumes; rigor:measured/tier:3 is conservative rather than inflated for an exhaustive deterministic count; and FR/ES translations are faithful across every sampled passage (opening mechanism paragraph, the global-check argument, the closing tie to no-forced-moves). On the extra load-bearing question: the page should stand alone, not fold. It has dense inbound citation from 6+ pages across three site sections (why/no-forced-moves, build/construct/beam-search, and three lab/experiments pages) plus the glossary's canonical 'see' target, and unique interactive/quantitative content no sibling page duplicates. The one substantive defect is a framing overclaim: the frontmatter source citation and metaDescription (identical in all three languages) assert that 'Régin's all-different reasoning predicts exactly where the starvation strikes,' but the page body never explains, demonstrates, or even mentions Régin, all-different constraints, bipartite matching, or Hall sets — the actual substantiation of that connection lives on a different page (build/reduce/alldiff-regin.mdx) that doesn't cross-link back here. A minor secondary issue is one ungrounded 'resisted so many attempts' aside. A minor structural note (not about this page's content, but about its neighbors) is that why/index.mdx's curated list omits piece-theft despite its citation weight.

border-balance.mdx is a well-sourced, well-linked hub page: the core NS-1/border-balance law and its provenance chain (angwin_uk 2007 -> mjqxxxx 2007 -> Owen 2007 -> Hopfer 2022) check out exactly against research/community-exports/messages.jsonl, the live Ns1Lab demo genuinely computes the deficit from real engine/WASM state, and FR/ES are faithful across all sampled passages, numbers, and links with no discrepancies. The page earns standalone status via six distinct inbound citations across three languages (glossary canonical 'see' target, people, known-facts, parity-arguments, rare-color-geography, hunt-part-2) so no fold is warranted. The substantive problem is that its two headline numeric claims -- pruning '10-28% of deep dead-ends' and '>=85%/over 85% of near-solution mismatches are interior-to-interior' -- have no committed backing anywhere in the repo: no research/topics/border-balance or ns1-deficit directory exists, no repro command, no LEDGER entry, and research/SITE_PLAN.md itself lists the ns1-deficit exact-recompute pipeline as still TODO. The page's repro:kind:prose tag is honestly declared, but these specific numeric ranges read as measured results to a reader, and the sibling page prune-vs-speed.mdx (a directly comparable pruning claim) shows the section's own fix already in use: a committed exact pipeline plus an explicit Callout disambiguating illustrative demo numbers from real measurements. That disambiguation is missing here, including for the toy 8x8/6-colour demo board being conflated with real-16x16 claims in the same paragraph.

prune-vs-speed.mdx is one of the strongest-written and best-calibrated pages in the section: it keeps its proven pure-math claim ((1/0.95)^256 ~ 5e5, verified exact) cleanly separated from its measured-single 6x6 hardness-curve data via an explicit closing Callout, in all three languages, and its two forum citations (msg 11848, 11856) are legitimately provenanced through the site's own citation-audit allowlist even though they postdate the local archive export. FR and ES siblings are faithful across all sampled passages, with identical frontmatter and no tier drift. The one real defect is a reproducibility-integrity bug: the page declares repro:exact against a committed results/prune-vs-speed.json that no longer reproduces from `just research-prune-vs-speed`, because two later engine commits (2026-07-19, 2026-07-21) changed the generator's colour-balance algorithm without regenerating this topic's committed artifact, and the site's CI repro-checker only verifies a repro: block is present, not that it actually reproduces, so the drift is silent. The page is heavily load-bearing (30+ inbound links across why/build/lab, order:10, the glossary's canonical 'pruning' target, and named outright as 'this site's core lesson' by several citing pages) and should not be folded into any parent.

The /research/why section is, on the whole, well-calibrated: exact internal counts (forbidden patterns, no forced moves, rare colours, piece theft) are genuinely proven-internal and reproducible; the hardness peak and complexity framing are correctly badged and externally corroborated; and the pages that are single-instance measurements (sigma-cycles, mismatch geometry, entropy area law) mostly say so. The one substantive tier mismatch is on the rigidity wall: it carries a rigor: proven badge and presents an MIP proof table as this project's exact computation, but only the SAT halo (radius 1-2) is committed under research/topics/; the MIP proofs are sourced to an external paper with no committed result on the site, so the proven tier overstates what a site reader can verify. Rigidity is nonetheless the best-replicated wall (SAT, MIP, annealing), while replication is thin everywhere else, a point the section's 'corroborated by the literature' language slightly oversells. Finally, four load-bearing community citations (msgs 11848, 11856, 11901, 11902) sit past the local archive's last message (11823) and could not be verified against the scoped archive, affecting rigidity's annealing corroboration, mismatch geometry's same-band citation, and prune-vs-speed's community verdict. The full wall-by-wall confidence map is written to undefined/cross-confidence-map.md, publication-ready for walls-and-methods or the section index.

The /research/why section presents its walls as parallel, and its rigor discipline on interdependence is sound: no page asserts a (c)-grade causal connection as fact. Every page that touches an open causal edge hedges it correctly — sigma-cycles.mdx calls its role "a plausible part of why the 470 record has stood" and flags the three-pair sample as unproven; entropy-area-law.mdx only observes the shared ~80-cell scale and links onward rather than claiming rigidity is caused by the area law; prune-vs-speed.mdx unifies the four walls thematically ("underneath, all the same statement: nothing local to prune"), which is a theme, not a causal reduction. The real gap is the opposite of over-claiming: two supportable-to-fact edges are under-linked (piece-theft as the cell-level face of the area law; forbidden-patterns as the exact small-patch scarcity beneath it), and three open causal edges (rigidity-from-area-law, no-forced-moves-from-the-peak, sigma-cycles-explaining-DFS-saturation) are not yet routed to /research/open-problems as questions. Full analysis, an interdependence table, publication-ready connective prose (states only a+b, routes c to open-problems), and three suggested open-problems entries are written to undefined/cross-interdependence.md. Verifiability aside: four load-bearing groups.io citations (msg 11848, 11856, 11901, 11902) fall beyond the local archive's range (max msg_num 11823) and could not be confirmed locally; the citations I could check (1947, 4909, 5027, 10045) all verify.

Both target pages get the complexity framing fundamentally right; there is no NP-completeness-implies-this-instance-is-hard fallacy on either page. how-hard-is-this-instance.mdx is a model treatment of the family/instance distinction: it correctly defines NP-completeness as a family property, calls a single instance 'a constant, not a problem,' states the both-directions caveat (a family can be NP-complete with trivial instances, and a polynomial family can have practically hard instances), and never infers instance hardness from the Demaine-Demaine theorem. The Demaine-Demaine 2007, Ansotegui 2008, and all six cited groups.io messages (256(1-1/e), 14702, 180-core-years, etc.) verify precisely against the paper record and local archive; the complex-theory page correctly labels itself conjectured, states its first-moment/independence/not-a-bound caveats, and keeps tree-width (~10^45) distinct from arrangement counts. The only substantive issue is one counting-caveat gap on how-hard: the 1.115e557 figure is the CONSTRAINED (border- and clue-pinned) arrangement count but is presented as raw 'search space width,' loosely equating an arrangement count with a search-tree width the site itself elsewhere puts at 10^45 — a minor one-clause fix, not a correctness defect. All findings are minor. Full read written to cross-complexity.md.

The /research/why section is well-populated and needs no folds and no new synthesis page — walls-and-methods already IS a strong closing synthesis (four-walls recap, master method-to-wall table with stall scores, per-method 'where it stopped', bridges to /build and /open-problems). Its real structural debt is integration and signalling, not page count. There is one true orphan (how-hard-is-this-instance, zero inbound links anywhere, yet substantial and distinct-audience — link it in, don't fold). The tier system covers only 10 of 16 pages and leaves the two heaviest hubs (walls-and-methods, prune-vs-speed) un-badged while a late-pruner leaf carries Tier-3, so the badge pattern reads as arbitrary. The index shows two contradicting 'start here' orderings on one screen (curated block vs auto card grid by `order`), and its 'structural walls' H2 ends as a dropped list (relying on an auto-injected card grid) while the `order` field buries the three headline walls the index names. No page clears the high fold bar: border-balance, hint-geometry, piece-theft, and mismatch-geometry all carry unique sourced evidence or outside-why demand. Full analysis written to undefined/cross-structure.md.

I deep-read EN + FR + ES for 8 why pages (the 4 mandatory ones plus mismatch-geometry, phase-transition, no-forced-moves, border-balance) and ran term/register/number greps across all 17 pages. Claim faithfulness is strong: every rigor badge (proven/measured/conjectured) matches EN, and every headline number is faithful in both languages — I verified the load-bearing ones against the community archive (msg 4909 gives 58%/8.5%/57%/88% and lengths 4-15 dead; msg 8924 gives 14,702; msg 8125 gives 256(1-1/e)=161.8; rigidity numbers 18700/429/432/48/225/28M all match). The defects are consistency and idiomaticity, not inverted results. The three that most undercut the 'written, not translated' standard: (1) FR splits the core noun 'board' into plateau vs grille across pages (sigma-cycles/mismatch-geometry/border-balance use grille, the rest plateau); (2) ES mixes tú/usted reader-address across pages while FR is cleanly 'vous'; (3) ES writes '14,702' with an English thousands-comma that a Spanish reader parses as a decimal. Remaining findings are minor per-page wobbles (a jammed dash in complex-theory.fr, 'scorer/scoring' anglicism in mismatch-geometry.fr, score/puntuación mixing, mismatch-term drift). Per-page notes written to /tmp/cross-i18n.md (the prompt's target dir was 'undefined').

surviving.description: The headline MIP proof table (lines 92-98: halo-1 through halo-4, McGavin 469, dual bound 123/116, board-wide bound <=476, 'out to halo-4', 'proven' badges) has NO committed results file anywhere in the repo. Grepping 895/929/1200/1800/18,700/halo-optimal/MIP-locally-optimal/'dual bound 123' across research/ returns only the private-paper reference and the 2026-07-17 audit's own quotation. The MIP sources[] entry points to a GitHub tree (/research), not a results artifact, and repro: kind: heavy carries no cmd/topic. The only committed, reproducible pipeline (research/topics/rigidity-sat-halo) backs the SAT half (C7: radius<=2 on 4 boards, all UNSAT) which is verified exact against results/halo-sat.json but is a WEAKER statement than the headline. So repro backs something weaker than the rigor:proven / tier:1 headline, and a site reader cannot check the load-bearing table. surviving.reason:
Full-context reread of border-balance.mdx (EN, and FR/ES sidecars, which mirror it exactly) confirms the reviewer's factual claims:

1. The ">85% of unmatched edges are interior-to-interior on 469-class boards" sentence has no inline citation, no footnote, and no reference to any of the five listed `sources:` (all five are groups.io posts about the border-balance law itself, none about a measured 85% figure on 469-class boards).
2. `grep` across the full eternity2 repo (web/content, research/topics, research/vault, research/LEDGER.md) finds exactly one occurrence of "85%" in the entire codebase: this sentence itself. No research/topics/<id>/ artifact exists, no LEDGER.md mention exists (confirmed by direct read — zero matches for border/NS-1/deficit).
3. The page's `repro: { kind: prose }` frontmatter, per the site's own CI script (`web/scripts/check-research-repro.mjs`, lines 6-9) and per `contribute.mdx`'s house rules ("Every number is reproducible or labelled for what it is... Seeded, stochastic, or heavy computations say so plainly"), specifically means "reproduces no number of its own (the measured figures it cites live on the linked experiment pages)." Border-balance links no such experiment page for the 85% figure — this is exactly the gap the convention exists to prevent, just not caught by the presence-only CI gate (which checks only that a `repro:` block exists, not that its prose claims are actually backed elsewhere).
4. Git history shows the 85% and the companion "10-28% dead-ends pruned" figure were introduced together in the initial content commit with no subsequent substantiation commit, and survived two dedicated audit passes (287aa94 "rigor badges + missing sources," 5342ec4 "pass 2 data-quality audit — all 700 citations verified") without either figure being sourced or flagged. This is a real, pre-existing gap, not something already handled.
5. The reviewer's parenthetical "same absence of backing" (implicitly comparing to another number on the page) also holds: the companion 10-28% pruner figure has an identical zero-backing profile, reinforcing that this is a real pattern on the page, not a one-off.

The proposed fix does not conflict with site conventions: it doesn't touch the metaDescription's em-dash (a frontmatter string field, not body prose, and consistent with other pages), doesn't require canonical-SOTA linking (border-balance is a structural finding, not a records/SOTA claim), and "label it as approximate/illustrative" is exactly the remediation pattern the site's own house rules and repro-prose convention prescribe for unreproduced measured numbers. No inbound anchors or links would break, since the fix is prose-level, not a structural/URL change.
surviving.corrected_fix:
Minor sharpening of the reviewer's proposed fix — both remedies are valid but should be scoped precisely:

Option A (in-page label, lowest-cost, matches house-rule precedent): Rewrite the sentence to explicitly flag it as an illustrative/approximate observation rather than a stated measurement, e.g. "on a run of 469-class boards, well over 80% of the unmatched edges sat interior-to-interior, invisible to NS-1" plus a source or an explicit "not yet a committed benchmark" qualifier — following the same "say so plainly" pattern contribute.mdx requires for seeded/stochastic results. This should be applied to BOTH the 85% claim AND the companion "10-28% of deep dead-ends" pruner figure two paragraphs later, since it has the identical zero-backing profile and the reviewer's underlying concern (unsourced measured numbers presented as fact) applies equally to it — a fix touching only the 85% sentence would leave the page half-fixed.

Option B (proper backing): Add a `research/topics/border-balance/` (or similarly named) reproducible artifact that computes both the interior-to-interior fraction and the pruning rate against a committed 469-class board set, cite it via the page's `sources:` list or inline, and change `repro.kind` from `prose` to `measured`/`seeded` per the site's own kind vocabulary — since `prose` is documented to mean "reproduces no number of its own," keeping `prose` while stating two specific percentages is itself the latent inconsistency underneath the reviewer's finding.

Either fix should apply uniformly to the EN, FR, and ES sidecars, since check-research-parity.mjs gates that they mirror each other, and both currently carry the identical unsourced figures.

surviving.description: The page carries the badge rigor: proven and presents a detailed MIP proof table (halo-1 joint McGavin 469 at 895s, Local 459 at 1800s, halo-3, halo-4 component-0 at 1200s, and a dual bound of 123 vs 116 implying a board-wide <=476) as this project's own exact computation. But the only rigidity result committed under research/topics/ is the SAT halo test (radius 1-2 on five boards, results/halo-sat.json). The MIP table is sourced only to an external 'project paper 2026-05-16' via a GitHub tree link, with no committed topic result on the site. The strongest form the site can actually stand behind is proven-internal for the SAT halo out to radius 2, plus proven-for-regions-closed-but-not-committed for the MIP. Presenting the MIP proofs at the same 'proven / this project's exact computation' tier as the committed SAT halo overstates what a reader can verify on the site.
surviving.proposed_fix: Either commit the MIP proof pipeline and results under research/topics/rigidity-* (matching the SAT-halo topic), or relabel the MIP table on the page as sourced to the external paper and distinct from the committed SAT-halo result. Keep rigor: proven only for the committed/closed regions and make the badge scope explicit; the 'all boards' generalisation is already correctly called a conjecture and should stay that way.
surviving.reason: Direct repo inspection confirms the claim. research/topics/ contains only one rigidity-related topic, rigidity-sat-halo (radius 1-2, actually 4 boards not 5 as the reviewer said — a minor inaccuracy that doesn't touch the substance). No MIP proof pipeline (no compute/ crate, no committed results/, no repro.topic) exists anywhere in the repo for the halo-1/halo-3/halo-4 MIP table (895s McGavin-469, 1800s Local-459, 929s halo-3, 1200s halo-4, dual bound 123 vs 116). The MIP table's only citation is sources[0], a bare link to the GitHub research tree labeled "project paper (2026-05-16)" — external provenance, not this repo's pipeline. This directly contradicts AGENTS.md's binding rule: "Every published result must be reproducible... published through the research/topics/<id>/ pipeline: a compute/ crate, committed results/, a copy-pasteable reproduce: command, and a GitHub 'computed from...' link." The SAT-halo claim on the same page correctly satisfies this (sources[3] links straight to the committed rigidity-sat-halo topic); the MIP claim does not, yet both are presented under the same rigor: proven badge and the same repro: {kind: heavy} block. A decisive cross-check: the sibling page sigma-cycles.mdx uses the identical bare repro: kind: heavy (no cmd, no topic) but is correctly badged rigor: conjectured, not proven — confirming the site's own convention ties "proven" to genuinely closed/committed evidence, and that rigidity-wall.mdx breaks that convention for the MIP portion. The page's existing hedge ("the general statement over all boards remains a conjecture," lines 106-108) scopes board-generalization, not the internal-vs-external evidentiary tier, so it does not already handle this finding, and the 2026-07-17 content-quality audit did not previously catch this specific issue either.
surviving.corrected_fix: Same two options the reviewer gave, both verified as available and low-risk (78 inbound links stay untouched, no anchor/URL change, no AGENTS.md style violation): (1) Preferred — commit the MIP proof pipeline under research/topics/rigidity-mip-halo/ (or similar), mirroring the exact structure already used for rigidity-sat-halo: a compute/ crate/script, committed results/ (a JSON table with the halo-1/halo-3/halo-4 regions, solver times, and dual bounds), and add repro.topic + a reproduce: command to the frontmatter so the "Code & data on GitHub" link resolves to a real committed result, matching AGENTS.md's reproducibility rule. (2) If re-running the MIP proofs is out of scope for this pass, downgrade sources[0]'s framing from "this project's own computation" and relabel the rigor scope explicitly on the page: keep rigor: proven only for the SAT-halo-backed claims (which are genuinely committed), and mark the MIP table rows as sourced to the external paper — e.g. add a one-line note above the table such as "computed in the project's private research notebook, not yet re-run under the site's committed-results pipeline" — so a reader isn't led to believe the 895s/1800s/929s/1200s runs are independently checkable from this repo the way the SAT-halo numbers are. Either way, leave the "general statement over all boards remains a conjecture" sentence untouched, since it correctly hedges a separate axis (board generalization) and is not implicated by this finding.

The reviewer's grep/check was scoped to web/content only, so the narrow "zero inbound prose links... in web/content" clause is technically accurate, but the generalized severity claim ("true orphan," reachability limited to sidebar+hub) is not — the page is one click from the primary nav on every page of the site, curated for precisely the CS-student/complexity-dispute audience the reviewer says it serves. The proposed fix (inbound prose links from complex-theory.mdx and prune-vs-speed.mdx, name it in index.mdx's framing cluster, don't fold) is directionally sound and matches site convention (AGENTS.md: cite another page's content via related[] site path + inline prose link at the claim). Nothing in the fix conflicts with parity, the no-em-dash/attribution-neutral gate, rigor enums, or canonical-SOTA linking (the page cites no SOTA numbers requiring /research/records linkage).
surviving.corrected_fix: Downgrade severity from major to minor/moderate and retitle the finding from "true orphan" to "no inbound links within the MDX content graph" (it is reachable and audience-matched via the site-wide nav's /start page, which already routes CS-student/complexity-question readers here explicitly — that channel should be credited, not ignored). Since neither target page currently discusses complexity classes, note that the fix requires drafting one to two new sentences, not just inserting a bare link, and must pass the em-dash/banned-phrase style gate and stay parity-mirrored across .fr.mdx/.es.mdx. Do not fold the page.

surviving.description: The index's final H2 'The structural walls' ends with a colon-style lead-in ('...the puzzle has measurable structure... surviving.proposed_fix: Either name the three headline walls (rigidity/entropy/forbidden) as bullets under that H2 to match the description, or reword the lead-in so it does not read as a promise of a missing list. Apply to all three language files.
surviving.corrected_fix: Prefer the reviewer's second option only, not the first: reword the "The structural walls" lead-in in all three language files (index.mdx, index.fr.mdx, index.es.mdx) so it stands as a complete, self-contained sentence that does not promise an enumeration -- matching the pattern already used by build/index.mdx and lab/index.mdx, which both close their prose with full standalone sentences rather than a colon-style lead-in. surviving.reason:
Verified directly against the repo on every point.

1. Text match: web/content/research/why/index.mdx (lines 60-64), index.fr.mdx (66-70), and index.es.mdx (62-66) all end their "structural walls" section with exactly the lead-in sentence the reviewer quotes ("...Each of these is published with the exact computation behind it.") and then the file terminates. No bullet list, no further prose. All three languages confirmed identical in structure.

2. The HubCards mechanism is real and matches the reviewer's description precisely. In web/src/components/docs/DocsShell.tsx, <HubCards doc={doc} /> renders unconditionally right after {children} (the MDX body). It checks doc.url === section.url (true for a section-root index) and renders section.items as a card grid. So on the live site the H2 is followed by an auto-injected grid, not a dropped list; read as raw MDX (e.g. via the .md sibling the page itself links), it genuinely dangles.

3. Cross-check against sibling section roots proves this is a real asymmetry, not just terse style: build/index.mdx ends on a complete, self-contained paragraph; lab/index.mdx ends on a complete "Add your own" paragraph; community/index.mdx ends inside an explicit <Door> grid that IS content in the MDX. None of the other three section roots end on a colon/promise-style sentence dependent on the auto-grid to read as non-broken. why/index.mdx is the outlier.

4. The description-vs-body gap is real but the reviewer's proposed fix needs correction: the section actually has four tier-1 pages (rigidity-wall, phase-transition, forbidden-patterns, sigma-cycles) plus about eleven more, and the auto-rendered HubCards grid shows all of them, not just three. walls-and-methods.mdx (linked one section up, in "Where to start") already owns the canonical "which wall stops which method" enumeration (four walls: forced moves, hardness peak, area law, rigidity). A hand-written bullet list of exactly "the three headline walls" would fork/duplicate that canonical taxonomy.

No qualifying context elsewhere on the page rescues the sentence.

surviving.description: The tier system is applied to only 10 of 16 pages, and the two heaviest hub pages are left un-badged. walls-and-methods (this page) is the section's synthesis spine — the index's #1 'start here', the bridge to the entire /build half, carrier of the master method-to-wall table, 9 inbound links — yet carries no `tier`, while border-balance (a late-search pruner) carries a Tier-3 badge. prune-vs-speed is likewise untiered despite being a top-3 hub (19 inbound, 12 from outside why/). The badge pattern reads as arbitrary: TierChips appear and vanish with no stated convention.
surviving.proposed_fix: Add `tier: 1` to walls-and-methods and prune-vs-speed (both flagship by weight/inbound), OR add a one-line convention note (in the index or a section doc) that exposition/framing pages are intentionally untiered so the missing badges are principled rather than silent.
surviving.reason:
All the factual load-bearing claims check out exactly as stated: 6 of 16 why/ pages lack `tier` (complex-theory, hint-geometry, how-hard-is-this-instance, mismatch-geometry, prune-vs-speed, walls-and-methods), walls-and-methods carries no tier while border-balance is Tier-3, walls-and-methods has exactly 9 inbound links and is literally listed first under "Where to start" in why/index.mdx ("The one-page map, and the place to orient first"), and prune-vs-speed has exactly 19 inbound links. No convention note exists anywhere (index.mdx, methodology.mdx, or a code comment) that principled the six missing badges.

However, two things weaken the finding as framed:

1. It is not "arbitrary" in the sense of unmotivated noise — there is a real, load-bearing distinction the reviewer didn't surface: `tier` in types.ts/DocsShell is documented as "editorial prominence" (1=Flagship/2=Finding/3=Supporting), a curation axis, NOT the rigor axis (proven/measured/conjectured) this review is otherwise scoped to. Confusingly, methodology.mdx's own "Axis 2: tier" section describes a *different* two-state concept (technical-report vs. reviewed-finding) that actually maps to the `status` frontmatter field, not the numeric `tier` field at all — so there is a real naming collision in the codebase between "tier" (status: report/live) and "tier" (numeric 1-3 prominence chip), and methodology.mdx documents the former while staying silent on the latter. That's a separate, arguably more important doc bug than "missing badges."

2. The gap is not a rigor-misrepresentation issue at all (nothing is presented as a higher evidence tier than it is — walls-and-methods correctly carries `rigor: conjectured` and `contribution: exposition`), so under this review's own severity framework (faulted for presenting lower tier as higher) this finding sits outside the core mandate; it's a pure editorial-consistency/UX nit, which argues for "minor," not "major."

The pattern is also not as clean a "flagship pages arbitrarily excluded" story as claimed: two `contribution: measurement`/`rigor: measured` findings (hint-geometry, mismatch-geometry) are also untiered despite being real scored findings, and no `kind`/`contribution` rule cleanly predicts the tier/no-tier split — this makes the omission look more like drift/inconsistent authoring discipline across many pages than a policy specifically starving the two heaviest hubs. The reviewer's diagnosis (arbitrary, no convention) is directionally correct but somewhat overstates it as a targeted problem with these two pages specifically.
surviving.corrected_fix:
Two-part fix, in this order:

1. First fix the terminology collision: methodology.mdx's "Axis 2: tier" section documents `status` (report/reviewed-finding) but is titled/worded as if it were the numeric `tier` field; rename that section's prose to clearly refer to publish status, and add a short paragraph (or a new "Axis 4") that actually documents the numeric `tier` frontmatter field (1=Flagship/2=Finding/3=Supporting, "editorial prominence within this section") including which pages/kinds it applies to.

2. Then, per that now-stated convention, either (a) add `tier: 1` to walls-and-methods.mdx and prune-vs-speed.mdx (and their .fr.mdx/.es.mdx siblings, required by check-research-parity.mjs's exact-frontmatter-match rule) since both are `kind: finding` with by far the highest inbound-link weight in the section, matching the existing tier-1 cohort (forbidden-patterns, sigma-cycles, phase-transition, rigidity-wall); or (b) state explicitly that `kind: finding` synthesis/framing pages are intentionally left untiered, and additionally reconcile the two other `kind: finding`/`rigor: measured` outliers (hint-geometry, mismatch-geometry) so the rule is demonstrably applied consistently, not just patched for the two pages the reviewer happened to flag.

Severity should be downgraded from "major" to "minor" — no evidence tier or rigor label is misrepresented; this is an editorial-consistency/documentation gap, not a rigor-mismatch under this review's core mandate.

surviving.description: FR cross-page terminology split for the core noun 'board'. 14 of 17 FR why-pages render board as 'plateau' (matching the glossary), but three pages render it as 'grille': sigma-cycles.fr (13 occurrences), mismatch-geometry.fr (20), border-balance.fr (8). The same fundamental term is inconsistent across the FR corpus, which reads as translated-per-page rather than written. ES is uniformly 'tablero' with no equivalent split. This is the most visible cross-page consistency defect in FR.
surviving.proposed_fix: Standardize FR 'board' to 'plateau' across sigma-cycles.fr, mismatch-geometry.fr and border-balance.fr (replace 'grille' → 'plateau' in prose; keep 'grille' only where the literal grid/lattice is meant, e.g. 'cuadrícula'-sense). Across the 17 FR why-pages, plateau is the dominant board-noun in 14 files (verified by full corpus scan); these 3 are the only files where grille dominates or is exclusive. The site's glossary.json — the authoritative terminology source, with ~40 board-related definitions — uses "plateau" exclusively for this concept and contains zero occurrences of "grille" anywhere, confirming plateau is the established convention, not an arbitrary majority. Checked for a legitimate distinct meaning that would justify "grille" (literal grid/lattice, idiomatic use like "grille de lecture/tarifaire"): none exists — every single grille occurrence in all three files refers to the same E2 record board referent that plateau denotes elsewhere (e.g. "Une grille résolue... la grille est fausse", "Deux grilles de haut niveau... l'autre grille", "Une grille quasi parfaite"). No style-guide or AGENTS.md rule sanctions "grille" as a deliberate technical distinction for these pages, and no existing tracking doc mentions this. surviving.reason:
The core finding survives verification but the reviewer's page inventory is wrong on one item and imprecise on another, which affects the fix's target list.

CONFIRMED:
- rigidity-wall.es.mdx uses usted throughout its two direct-address passages: "Intente superarlo usted mismo" (heading), "Mueva cualquier cosa en el entorno" — genuine usted imperatives (usted-form "mueva," not tú-form "mueve").
- phase-transition.es.mdx uses usted: "O mídalo usted mismo" (heading), "en su navegador," plus later "usted mismo puede observar" — consistent usted register.
- design-recipe.es.mdx DOES carry usted register, contra what a naive grep for the literal word "usted" would show (zero hits). Its two imperatives are usted-conjugated: "Hágalo demasiado fácil..." / "Hágalo literalmente imposible..." (usted-form of hacer; tú-form would be "Hazlo") and "Redondee a 17..." (usted-form of redondear; tú-form would be "Redondea"). So the reviewer's inclusion of design-recipe.es is correct, just under-evidenced by a literal-string search.
- walls-and-methods.es.mdx, mismatch-geometry.es.mdx, complex-theory.es.mdx all confirmed tú-register: "elige tu nicho"/"elige el muro" (walls-and-methods), "Elige un tablero" (mismatch-geometry), "Toma un orden," "Multiplica eso," "Encadena este cálculo," "Lanza el barrido" (complex-theory) — all tú-form imperatives.
- FR sibling set is confirmed clean: grep for tu/ton/ta/tes/toi across all 17 FR why-pages returns zero true positives (the two raw hits in design-recipe.fr.mdx are "tué," past participle of tuer, a false match, not tutoiement) versus consistent "vous"/"votre" everywhere reader address occurs.

REFUTED in part — one item in the reviewer's usted list is wrong:
- no-forced-moves.es.mdx has exactly one direct-address sentence in its entire body: "Coloca esto junto a los patrones prohibidos..." ("Por qué importa" section). "Coloca" is the TÚ-form imperative of colocar (usted-form would be "Coloque"). The reviewer listed this page among the usted group; it actually belongs, if anything, in the tú camp — it is not evidence of usted drift, and citing it weakens rather than strengthens the "formal vs informal split across pages" claim, though it doesn't erase the split since three other pages (rigidity-wall, phase-transition, design-recipe) still cleanly demonstrate genuine usted usage against six-plus confirmed tú pages elsewhere in the section.

No convention doc, CI style gate, or i18n rule in AGENTS.md governs ES formal/informal register (checked AGENTS.md in full and check-research-style.mjs's documented scope — it only gates em dashes, banned phrases, and msg-number citations, nothing about tú/usted). So the inconsistency is real, unaddressed by existing tooling, and not already "handled" anywhere. The proposed fix (standardize on tú, site-wide across why/) does not touch anchors, frontmatter, rigor enums, sources[], or the canonical-SOTA link to /research/records, since it is pure body-prose register conversion — no structural risk. It also matches the site's own stated voice goal ("accessible & human... first-person researcher voice") better than usted would.
surviving.corrected_fix:
Standardize ES why-section reader address on tú (informal), matching the site's accessible-voice convention and the majority pattern already in the section. Concretely convert:
1. rigidity-wall.es.mdx — "Intente superarlo usted mismo" → "Inténtalo tú mismo" (heading); "Mueva cualquier cosa" → "Mueve cualquier cosa."
2. phase-transition.es.mdx — "O mídalo usted mismo" → "O mídelo tú mismo" (heading); "en su navegador" → "en tu navegador"; and the later "usted mismo puede observar" → "tú mismo puedes observar."
3. design-recipe.es.mdx — "Hágalo demasiado fácil..." → "Hazlo demasiado fácil..."; "Hágalo literalmente imposible..." → "Hazlo literalmente imposible..."; "Redondee a 17..." → "Redondea a 17..." (these are the load-bearing usted markers, not the literal string "usted" — a fix pass must grep for usted-conjugated verb endings, not just the pronoun, or it will miss this page exactly as the original naive audit did).

Drop no-forced-moves.es.mdx from the conversion list — its sole direct-address imperative, "Coloca esto junto a los patrones prohibidos," is already tú-form and needs no change; including it in the fix list was based on a misreading (likely confusing third-person possessive "su" elsewhere on the page, which refers to the puzzle/piece, not the reader, with formal address).

After conversion, re-run a project-wide grep for usted-conjugated imperative endings (not just the literal word "usted") across all ES why-pages to catch any remaining instances the literal-string search would miss, then spot-check the FR siblings remain untouched (they are already uniformly vous and out of scope).

minors.description: The page badge is rigor: measured, which is appropriate, but the 'theorem, briefly' section pairs an external existence result (Fekete subadditivity gives the limit) with a single-instance measured value (h_infinity near 0.67, and the area-law decay exp(-alpha n^2) with alpha about 0.085). The theorem guarantees a positive limit exists and is bounded by 1.6645; it does not fix 0.67 or 0.085. A reader could take the theorem framing as certifying the specific numbers.
minors.proposed_fix: Add one line separating the proven part (a positive limit exists, bounded above by log10(46.18)=1.6645) from the measured part (its value near 0.67 and alpha about 0.085 are this project's single-instance fit, extrapolated past the exactly counted widths). No number change needed.

minors.description: The method table lists best scores (PALIMPSEST 463/480, PRIOR 460, KEYRING 460, etc.) without stating the scoring convention, while the site's records page and the project's own record are strict-5/5 (461) versus matched-edges (463). The 463 shown for PALIMPSEST is a matched-edges figure; the ScoringPrimer and records pages foreground the 470 community ceiling and strict-460 conventions. A reader comparing the 463 in this table to a strict-460 elsewhere may conflate two conventions.
minors.proposed_fix: Add a one-line note under the table (or a column header footnote) stating which scoring convention the 'Best' column uses (matched edges out of 480) and link to the ScoringPrimer / records page, so the 463/460 figures are not read as strict-5/5.

minors.description: Edge 1 (supportable, (b)-tier, currently unstated): piece theft is the cell-level face of the entropy area law. Both pages independently name the use-each-piece-once rule as the seat of hardness (piece-theft: 'Globally the supply is fine; the failure is one scarce piece misallocated'; entropy-area-law: 'the quiet rule that each piece is used once, whose cost grows with area'), but they never cross-link and never state that they are the same use-once constraint at two scales. This is a definitional identity, not a conjecture, so it can be stated as fact without upgrading either page's 'measured' tier.
minors.proposed_fix: Add /research/why/entropy-area-law to piece-theft.mdx's `related` frontmatter (and reciprocally piece-theft to entropy-area-law), and add one connective sentence naming piece theft as the local mechanism of the area-law distinctness collapse. Use the connective prose drafted in undefined/cross-interdependence.md.

minors.description: Three (c)-grade causal edges are plausible but unproven and are not routed anywhere as open questions: (1) is local rigidity a consequence of the area-law distinctness collapse, given both share a ~80-cell scale; (2) is the 73-137 branching factor (no-forced-moves) forced by sitting at the ~17-colour one-solution peak; (3) does the sigma-cycle wall (computed only between finished record boards, three-pair sample) also explain constructive DFS/beam saturation, not just local-search saturation. None is asserted as fact on-site (correct), but none is captured as a question either.
minors.proposed_fix: Add three open-problems entries phrased as questions with their walls named, per the 'Suggested open-problems entries' section of undefined/cross-interdependence.md. Frame each as 'is X a consequence of Y, or a coincidence of scale', keeping them at conjecture tier.

minors.description: Edge 3: forbidden-patterns and the area law are already cross-linked and are both exact small-patch scarcity counts, so the 'same phenomenon at different scales' edge is (b)-supportable. But the clean form over-simplifies: forbidden-patterns counts colour-match feasibility of placements (2x2 99.72% forbidden by matching alone), whereas the area law's exp(-alpha n^2) decay is specifically the use-once overlay applied on top of colour-valid patches, where colour-matching is the 'generous' rule. Any added connective prose must not conflate the colour-match axis with the distinctness axis.
minors.proposed_fix: If connective tissue is added, state the narrower true claim: both are exact exhaustive counts showing realizable small patches vanish super-linearly with size, and the area law isolates use-once as the rule doing the collapsing. Do not describe forbidden-patterns as 'the area law in the small' without that distinction.

minors.description: The 1.115x10^557 figure (lines 72, 93, 216) is presented as 'distinct piece-and-rotation arrangements' and 'the search space is ~10^557 wide', but the site's own derivation (build/known-facts.mdx:170) shows this is the CONSTRAINED count: 4!·56!·195!·4^195, i.e. corners in corners, edges on the border with orientation forced, and clue pinned. The unconstrained count is the other row, 256!·4^256 ≈ 10^661 (~10^104 larger). The page gives no hint that 10^557 is the border-and-clue-pinned figure rather than the raw one, so a specialist who knows 256!·4^256 ≈ 10^661 sees a smaller number and is not told why.
minors.proposed_fix: On first use (line 72) qualify the count as the border-aware, clue-pinned arrangement count, e.g. 'on the order of 1.115x10^557 distinct arrangements once corners, edges, and the clue are pinned (the unconstrained figure is ~10^661)', cross-linking build/known-facts for the derivation.

minors.description: The phrase 'the search space is ~10^557 wide' (lines 93 and 216) equates a static ARRANGEMENT count with search-tree WIDTH. These are different objects: the complex-theory page correctly puts the search tree's widest point at ~10^45 ways to extend, not 10^557. This is the one place a complexity theorist would want the arrangements-vs-search-states distinction drawn, and it is the distinction the sibling complex-theory page otherwise keeps scrupulously. The claim 'wide space, nearly empty solution set' is directionally true, so this is a wording slide, not a false claim.
minors.proposed_fix: Replace 'the search space is ~10^557 wide' with a phrasing that names the object, e.g. '~10^557 candidate arrangements over a nearly empty solution set' (a count, not a tree width), and note that a good scan order never visits that many states — the tree's widest point is ~10^45 (link complex theory).

minors.description: The funnel-sweep prose (lines 104-108) describes the animated counter as climbing 'into the billions' before the plateau, but the Three-regimes section (lines 126-136) puts the plateau width at ~10^45. 'Billions' (10^9) undersells the stated plateau by ~36 orders of magnitude. This describes a UI animation rather than asserting a numeric claim, but a reader who reads 'billions' then '10^45' a few paragraphs later may be briefly confused about which figure the animation displays.
minors.proposed_fix: Either reconcile the animation's displayed scale with the 10^45 plateau, or change 'climbs into the billions' to a scale-agnostic phrase (e.g. 'climbs astronomically') so the counter narration does not name a magnitude that contradicts the regime table.

minors.description: The `order` field buries the index's own headline walls. The index description names 'rigidity, entropy, forbidden patterns' as the three measurable walls, but entropy-area-law is ordered last (130, behind border-balance and piece-theft) and forbidden-patterns (80) sits behind supporting-tier material, while rigidity-wall (the stated primary wall, Tier-1, 23 inbound) is at order 50, after design-recipe (45, a Tier-2 reconstruction). The reading order does not match the argumentative priority the index asserts.
minors.proposed_fix: Pull entropy-area-law and forbidden-patterns forward in `order` so the three headline walls named in the index description read early in the walls block; move rigidity-wall ahead of design-recipe.

minors.description: sigma-cycles is marked tier:1 (flagship) but is rigor:conjectured, repro:heavy, and has 10 inbound links — it functions as a companion to rigidity-wall (both cite McGavin 469 / msg 10045, both about record-board rigidity) rather than a standalone flagship. Meanwhile the genuinely flagship-weight synthesis (walls-and-methods) and root explainer (prune-vs-speed) carry no tier at all, so the Tier-1 set over-weights a companion page and under-weights the hubs.
minors.proposed_fix: Consider demoting sigma-cycles to tier:2 (companion to rigidity-wall), and group rigidity-wall/sigma-cycles/mismatch-geometry as an adjacent 'record-board anatomy' cluster by order.

minors.description: hint-geometry sits mid-walls (order 95) but has 0 inbound links from within the why/ section (all 3 inbound come from one external experiment cluster, raphael-anjou/hint-study). It lists no-forced-moves and phase-transition as `related`, but the prose of those pages does not link back, so within-section it is an island despite a distinct finding (position beats count; McGavin 18-hint solve, msgs 11725/11746 present in archive). Keep the page; improve integration.
minors.proposed_fix: Add a within-why prose inbound link (from no-forced-moves and/or phase-transition, which it already lists as related) so it is contextually reachable inside the section, not only from the hint-study experiment pages.

minors.description: Line 117 has a missing space after a closing parenthetical dash: '...à travers le plateau - cette reptation plate au sein d'une bande astronomiquement large, c'est le mur -avant que les soixante dernières pièces...'. 'le mur -avant' is jammed (no space before 'avant'), rendering as a visible typo. The EN uses commas, not dashes, for this appositive.
minors.proposed_fix: Insert a space: '...c'est le mur - avant que...' (or, better, follow the EN and use commas: '...c'est le mur, avant que les soixante dernières pièces ne le fassent redescendre en entonnoir.').

minors.description: Anglicism/idiomaticity: this FR page renders 'score' as the calque 'scorer/scoring' ('Scorez une grille', 'notre façon de scorer', 'la règle de scoring') where sibling FR pages use 'noter'/'note'. Combined with its use of 'grille' for board, the page reads more literally translated than the rest of the FR corpus. Numbers are all faithful (469, 11, rows 0-4/5-15, 249/256, 7 holes rows 1-4).
minors.proposed_fix: Replace 'Scorez'→'Notez', 'façon de scorer'→'façon de noter', 'règle de scoring'→'règle de notation' (or keep 'scoring' as a noun consistently with other pages), and 'grille'→'plateau' per the corpus term fix.

minors.description: Within-page term wobble in ES: 'score' is rendered both as 'score' (line 8 metaDescription 'donde cala', line 32 'el score exacto') and as 'puntuación' (lines 63, 99, 159). The FR sibling uses 'score' consistently. Not a claim drift, purely terminological consistency.
minors.proposed_fix: Standardize to 'puntuación' throughout walls-and-methods.es (the dominant ES term across the corpus), or keep 'score' consistently; do not mix within one page.

minors.description: FR term drift for 'mismatch': phase-transition.fr uses 'non-concordance', mismatch-geometry.fr uses 'désaccord', rigidity-wall.fr uses 'discordance'. Three different renderings of the same concept across FR pages. The glossary lists 'break/rupture' but no single settled FR word for the mismatch concept, so pages diverged.
minors.proposed_fix: Settle on one FR term for 'mismatch' (recommend 'désaccord', already used on the dedicated page 'Où vivent les désaccords') and apply it consistently across phase-transition.fr, rigidity-wall.fr and mismatch-geometry.fr; add it to glossary.json FR terms.

minors.description: metaDescription drops an EN precision hedge: EN 'an exact multiset equality' becomes FR 'mêmes multiensembles' (the 'exact' qualifier is lost in the meta only; the body retains full precision with the Δ definition). Low impact but a small tier/precision softening in a search-facing string.
minors.proposed_fix: Restore the qualifier in the FR metaDescription, e.g. 'égalité exacte de multiensembles', to match the EN 'exact multiset equality'.
